title: The Doctor Who Saved Sight
body:
## The Doctor Who Saved Sight

Patricia Bath grew up in New York City in the 1940s. As a girl, she loved science. Her mother bought her a chemistry set, even though most kids at that time did not get one. Patricia spent hours mixing harmless liquids and writing down what she saw.

In high school, Patricia won a science fair award for her work on cancer cells. She was just sixteen years old. The award helped her get into college. Later, she went to medical school. She wanted to help people see.

## A Hard Path

When Patricia became an eye doctor in 1973, very few Black women were doctors. Patricia was the first Black eye doctor at her university hospital. She often had to work harder than other doctors to be heard. She did not give up.

She noticed something important. Many of her older patients were going blind from a condition called cataracts. A cataract is a cloudy spot that forms over the lens of the eye. The cloudy spot blocks the light. People with cataracts see the world like they are looking through fog.

Patricia wanted a better way to remove cataracts. The old way used a small blade. The blade worked, but it was rough on the eye.

## The Laserphaco Probe

In 1981, Patricia began work on a new tool. It used a tiny laser to break up the cloudy spot. Then it washed the pieces away with water. This made the surgery faster and gentler than the old way. Patients felt better the next day instead of weeks later.

It took Patricia five years to finish her invention. In 1988, she received a patent for the tool. A patent is a paper that protects a new invention. Patricia was the first Black female doctor to get a medical patent in the United States.

Her invention has helped doctors all over the world restore sight to people who were going blind. Today, doctors still use ideas from her work.

## A Lasting Legacy

Patricia Bath did more than invent a new tool. She also taught younger doctors. She started a group of doctors who travel to places where eye care is hard to find. The group helps people in small towns and far away countries. They bring eye exams, glasses, and surgery to people who could not get them before.

Patricia Bath proved one big thing. A curious girl with a chemistry set can grow up to change how the world sees. Her story shows that asking questions and not giving up can lead to big inventions.
